items.q: What does it mean that RAM is volatile?
items.correct: Its contents are lost when power is removed.
items.misconception: It can explode or be damaged easily.
items.why: Students take 'volatile' from chemistry literally. In computing, volatile means contents don't persist without power - that's why RAM is used for working memory, not storage.

items.q: What happens when 11111111 + 1 is computed in an 8-bit register?
items.correct: Overflow - the result requires 9 bits but only 8 are available.
items.why: Students miss that registers have fixed width. The mathematical result is 9 bits, but the 8-bit register drops the carry-out, producing 00000000 with an overflow flag.

items.q: How many layers are in the OSI model?
items.correct: 7 (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
items.why: Students confuse OSI (7 layers) with the TCP/IP model (4 layers). They are related but distinct - OSI is a theoretical reference model; TCP/IP is the practical Internet stack.
It's RAM. “ROM” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which storage has no moving parts?
items.why: Correct answer: SSD. “HDD” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many bytes are in 1 kilobyte (decimal)?
items.why: Exact answer: 1000 bytes. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
